Discovery.Purpose: First session of the SDD flow. Capture operator's intent and target consumers, gate requirements and constraints, propose 2-3 design variants scaled by project type (app / service-module-sdk / cli-utility), gate the architecture choice and record the operator's rationale in a Decision Log. Output: a root-level project spec at `specs/<root>.spec.md`.
Discovery.Triggers: - Operator describes a raw idea: new feature, new module, new service, SDK, CLI utility, or app. - Operator asks to "design", "discuss the architecture", "think through how to build". - Substantial refactor that re-evaluates module boundaries or system responsibilities. - No `specs/` directory exists for the discussed scope, or root spec is missing.
Discovery.SkipWhen: - An approved root spec at `specs/<root>.spec.md` already exists for this scope. - Request is a bug fix, performance fix, or local refactor inside an existing module. - Operator asks for a targeted change in existing code without re-evaluating architecture.
Discovery.Keywords: discovery, intent, vision, golden DX example, design variants, project type, architecture choice, decision log, requirements gate, raw idea
Discovery.Preconditions: - None: this is the entry point of the SDD flow.

Examples.Positive: - "I want to build an SDK for event analytics." - "Let's design a CRUD service for order management." - "Think through the architecture of a CLI utility for migrations."
Examples.Negative: - "Fix a bug in the auth service." - "Rewrite function X using async/await." - "Add an email field to the existing contract."

DomainModeling.Purpose: Second session of the SDD flow. Decompose the project into a hierarchy of domains and produce a connected tree of domain specs at `specs/<domain>/<domain>.spec.md`. For each domain: enumerate ALL entities (closed-world inventory), describe each entity's public surface (properties, methods, internal/external consumers), formalize DbC contracts (Ports and Adapters), and design the file structure with implementation rules.
DomainModeling.Triggers: - Discovery session is complete; root spec at `specs/<root>.spec.md` is approved. - Operator moves to module decomposition, entity inventory, or contract formalization. - Operator says "split into domains", "build the module map", "list all entities", "produce DbC", "split Ports and Adapters".
DomainModeling.SkipWhen: - Root spec is missing or not approved. - Discovery is still open, design variants or architecture not yet frozen. - Task tickets already exist — domain modeling belongs earlier in the flow.
DomainModeling.Keywords: domain decomposition, module map, hierarchy, entity inventory, closed-world, public surface, consumers, DbC, contracts, Ports, Adapters, file structure, dev rules
DomainModeling.Preconditions: - An approved root spec at `specs/<root>.spec.md` exists.

Examples.Positive: - "Architecture is approved, split it into domains." - "Build the entity inventory for the orders domain." - "Formalize Ports and Adapters across all domains."
Examples.Negative: - "The idea is raw; we still need to discuss what we are building." - "Tasks are already generated; let's start coding."

TaskScaffolding.Purpose: Third session of the SDD flow. Build a DAG of self-contained task tickets at `tasks/<scope>/<scope>.task-NN.md`. Each ticket MUST explicitly reference: relevant spec sections, dependencies on other tasks, applicable `coding/<lang>-rules.xml`, applicable `testing/<framework>.xml`, BDD scenarios with canonical case names, verification commands, an execution log template, and `@task`/`@purpose` file-header rules. If coding or testing rules for the chosen stack do not exist, the directive must abort — task cannot be self-contained without them.
TaskScaffolding.Triggers: - Domain modeling is complete; all domain specs have contracts and file structures. - Operator asks to decompose specs into tasks. - Operator says "break into tasks", "build the DAG", "produce the execution plan", "scaffold tickets".
TaskScaffolding.SkipWhen: - Domain specs lack contracts (Ports/Adapters not formalized). - Discovery or domain modeling is not yet closed. - Required `coding/<lang>-rules.xml` or `testing/<framework>.xml` for the chosen stack is missing — abort with a clear failure, do not generate partial tickets. - Tasks are already generated; operator wants a targeted edit to an existing ticket.
TaskScaffolding.Keywords: tasks, DAG, decomposition, task tickets, scaffolding, BDD scenarios, execution plan, canonical case names, verification commands, execution log
TaskScaffolding.Preconditions: - A complete spec hierarchy under `specs/` exists, including domain-level contracts. - `coding/<lang>-rules.xml` exists for every language used in the project. - `testing/<framework>.xml` exists for every test runner used in the project.

Examples.Positive: - "Specs are ready, break them into tasks." - "Scaffold task tickets for all Adapters in the orders domain." - "Build the DAG of execution tasks across the spec tree."
Examples.Negative: - "Architecture is still under discussion." - "There are no contracts yet." - "We don't have coding rules for the target language yet."

Audit.Purpose: Post-execution verification session. After a task (or an epic) is executed by an implementation agent, audit compares spec ↔ task ↔ actual code and detects: (1) closed-world drift — entities present in code but absent from spec; (2) completeness gaps — promised behavior not implemented; (3) runtime fidelity violations — `runtime-hook-required` claims backed only by simulation; (4) coding/testing rules compliance violations; (5) insights to backflow into specs. Output: an audit report at `audits/<task-id>.audit.md` with findings and proposed remediations.
Audit.Triggers: - A task ticket has been executed and its `Execution Log` is filled in. - Operator asks for "audit", "drift detection", "post-implementation review", "verify the implementation". - End of an epic / batch of tasks, before merging or shipping.
Audit.SkipWhen: - No executed tasks to audit (Execution Log is empty). - Implementation is still in progress within the same task. - No spec to compare against (audit requires spec ↔ task ↔ code triangulation).
Audit.Keywords: audit, post-execution review, drift detection, closed-world fidelity, completeness, runtime fidelity, code review, insight backflow, remediation
Audit.Preconditions: - The relevant spec(s) under `specs/` exist. - The task ticket under `tasks/` exists with a non-empty `Execution Log`. - The implementation code referenced by the ticket is committed and accessible.

Examples.Positive: - "Task TSK-12 is done, run the audit." - "Audit the orders epic for drift before we merge." - "Verify that the implementation matches the spec for module X."
Examples.Negative: - "The task is still in progress." - "There's no spec yet, just a prototype."

TypeScriptCodingRules.Purpose: Canonical rules for writing TypeScript code in this project: typing, style, patterns, error handling, anti-patterns, verification hooks.
TypeScriptCodingRules.Triggers: - Execution agent is about to create or modify TypeScript code. - Task ticket declares the implementation language as TypeScript. - Files use `.ts`, `.tsx`, `.mts`, or `.cts` extensions. - Project configuration includes `tsconfig.json` and the task touches compiled modules.
TypeScriptCodingRules.SkipWhen: - Task does not modify TypeScript code (docs only, YAML configs only, shell scripts only). - Target module's stack is a different language (Rust, Python, Go, Swift, Kotlin, etc.).
TypeScriptCodingRules.Keywords: TypeScript, TS, .ts, .tsx, .mts, .cts, interfaces, types, tsc, tsconfig, type narrowing, generics, enum, async/await
TypeScriptCodingRules.Preconditions: - Loaded into the executor's context BEFORE writing or editing TypeScript code. - Task ticket must explicitly reference this file as a mandatory coding rules dependency.

Examples.Positive: - "Implement the PaymentGateway interface in TypeScript." - "Write the OrderProcessor class against the contract from the spec." - "Fix typing in module X."
Examples.Negative: - "Rewrite the deploy script in bash." - "Implement the module in Rust." - "Update README.md."

NodeTestRules.Purpose: Canonical rules for writing tests on the built-in `node:test` runner: file layout, naming, describe/test structure, assertions, mocking, coverage, mapping BDD scenarios to test cases.
NodeTestRules.Triggers: - Execution agent is about to create or modify tests on Node's built-in runner. - Task ticket declares the test framework as `node:test`. - Project's test command is `node --test` (no separate runner like Vitest or Jest). - Test files follow `*.test.ts` / `*.test.js` convention under Node without third-party test runners.
NodeTestRules.SkipWhen: - Tests target a different runner (Vitest, Jest, Mocha, Playwright, Cypress, etc.). - Task does not include writing or modifying tests. - Tests are end-to-end under a browser runner.
NodeTestRules.Keywords: node:test, node --test, test runner, assertions, describe, it, test cases, mock, BDD, canonical case names, coverage
NodeTestRules.Preconditions: - Loaded into the executor's context BEFORE writing tests. - Task ticket must explicitly reference this file as a mandatory testing rules dependency.

Examples.Positive: - "Write `node:test` tests for the OrderProcessor module." - "Cover the BDD scenarios from the ticket with tests on Node's built-in runner." - "Add contract tests for PaymentGatewayPort using `node:test`."
Examples.Negative: - "Write tests using Vitest." - "Build end-to-end tests with Playwright." - "Cover the UI component with Jest snapshots."
